rationale: The citing paper uses Bolton and Reed (2004) for more than background listing. It explicitly relies on that paper as prior evidence that an activated identity can be suppressed via instruction, and uses this logic to motivate H3 and the design of Study 4 on consumer-identity suppression. It also cites Bolton and Reed in the mechanism argument that identity activation increases accessibility of personal preferences. This is a meaningful conceptual input to hypothesis development and interpretation.
relation_description: The citing paper extends Bolton and Reed's identity-based judgment line by transferring its identity activation/suppression logic to managerial empathy and consumer preference prediction. It tests whether identity suppression instructions can similarly reduce self-referential bias in managers' judgments.
